title: Your only chance of getting into Stanford is to start a business
html:
TL;DR

  • September 2019 title-as-content note: "You're only chance of getting into Stanford is to start a business." Body is empty — the title is the entire thought.
  • The earliest dated Stanford-as-target line in the corpus, and the first explicit startup-as-admissions-strategy framing.
  • 16-year-old Yahir is doing the GP rural kid math: top-1% private-school applicants don't beat me on grades and SAT alone, but a real business is a moat.
  • Sits one day after 2019 09 25 feeding illusion or ending it — same week he's negotiating what's the real path vs. the illusion.
  • Foreshadows the eventual OSU landing (no Stanford) but more importantly, it shows the startup-as-credential logic that recurs through bromenu, rankett, and the AI search thesis arc.

Notes

The note is a single sentence with no body — the kind of manifesto style one-liner Yahir uses when an aphorism arrives whole and doesn't need elaboration. That it never got expanded is the data: the teen wasn't taking notes for himself to re-read, he was stamping the conviction down so it would persist.

The diagnosis embedded in "only chance" is interesting and a little bleak: 16-year-old Yahir has already concluded that the conventional credential-path (grades, SAT, APUSH, FBLA) won't be enough from GP. He needs a thing — a business — to break through. This is the earliest structural articulation of what becomes his lifelong pattern: you can't out-credential the credentialed; you have to build.

He doesn't get into Stanford. But the "start a business" part of the prescription stays — five years later it's bromenu, seven years later it's rankett and liftatx strategy. The aphorism outlived the target.

Privacy note

None.
